m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is inorder traversal of the following tree?
            9
         /    \
        3     11
       / \    / \
      2   7  14  15
     /   /
   1   5
m_Item1.Explanation:
Start at the root node (9).
Traverse the left subtree of 9:
Traverse the left subtree of 3:
Traverse the left subtree of 2:
Visit node 1 (left subtree of 2 is null, so visit 1).
Visit node 2 (after visiting its left subtree).
The right subtree of 2 is null.
Visit node 3 (after visiting its left subtree).
Traverse the right subtree of 3:
Traverse the left subtree of 7:
Visit node 5 (left subtree of 5 is null, so visit 5).
Visit node 7 (after visiting its left subtree).
The right subtree of 7 is null.
Visit the root node 9 (after visiting its left subtree).
Traverse the right subtree of 9:
Traverse the left subtree of 11:
Visit node 14 (left subtree of 14 is null, so visit 14).
The right subtree of 14 is null.
Visit node 11 (after visiting its left subtree).
Traverse the right subtree of 11:
Visit node 15 (left subtree of 15 is null, so visit 15).
The right subtree of 15 is null.

100 , 20 , 10 , 30 , 200 , 150 , 300
InOrder

m_Item1.QuestionText: Maximum number of nodes in a binary tree with height k, where root is height 0, is
m_Item1.Explanation:
The maximum number of nodes in a binary tree with height k, where the root height 0 is, 2k+1 − 1.
2h-1 is the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h. The maximum number of nodes on the root-to-leaf path is given by height. The height of a tree with one node is 1 in this case. The number of leaf nodes in a binary tree with 0 or 2 children is always one more than the number of leaf nodes in a binary tree with two children.

​

m_Item1.QuestionText: If we choose Prim's Algorithm for uniquely weighted spanning tree instead of Kruskal's Algorithm, then

Options.OptionText: we'll get a different spanning tree.
we'll get the same spanning tree.
spanning will not cover all vertices.

m_Item1.Explanation: Regardless of which algorithm is used, in a graph with unique weight, resulting spanning tree will be same.

}
m_Item1.Explanation: sizeof(arr) will be total size allocated for that array (=16 bytes)& sizeof(arr[0]) is a size allocated for 1 single element(float & is 4 bytes), will give no of elements in array = 4.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In-order, pre-order and post-order can be applied to

Options.OptionText: any trees other than binary trees

m_Item1.Explanation: All traversal methods are applicable to any Binary Trees

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the recursive traversing of post-order traversal

Options.OptionText: traverse the left sub-tree, visit the root node and traverse the right sub-tree
visit the root node, traverse the left sub-tree, and traverse the right sub-tree
traverse the left sub-tree, traverse the right sub-tree, and visit the root node

m_Item1.Explanation: Inorder is left data right (LDR), preorder is data,left and right (DLR), postorder is left,right,data (LRD).

m_Item1.QuestionText: The number of different directed trees with 3 nodes are______________
m_Item1.Explanation:
This is because the there are 3 possible ways of putting one of the trees first. Then, since you have 2 trees left to order there are 2 ways of putting one of the trees second and then there is only one tree left and only one place to put it.
So is 3*2 = 6

m_Item1.QuestionText: One can convert a binary tree into its mirror image by traversing it in
m_Item1.Explanation:
Traverse left subtree of node 1 (root):
Traverse left subtree of node 2:
Traverse left subtree of node 4 (leaf node): No left or right child, so nothing to swap.
Traverse right subtree of node 4: No right child, so nothing to swap.
Swap left and right children of node 4: No effect since it's a leaf.
Traverse right subtree of node 2:
Traverse left subtree of node 5 (leaf node): No left or right child, so nothing to swap.
Traverse right subtree of node 5: No right child, so nothing to swap.
Swap left and right children of node 5: No effect since it's a leaf.
Swap left and right children of node 2:

                 1
                / \
               2  3
              / \ / \
            4  5 6  7
 So we will get mirror image with postorder
                1
               / \
             3   2
           / \   / \
         7   6 5   4

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which data structure is commonly used to represent hierarchical data observed in file systems?
m_Item1.Explanation: A Binary Tree is a hierarchical data structure suitable for representing file systems, where each parent node can have at most two child nodes representing directories and files.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which type of tree is most beneficial for search operations, such as querying a database?
m_Item1.Explanation: B-Trees are commonly used in databases to provide efficient search, sequential access, insertions, and deletions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which tree is best suited for autocomplete features in text editors?
m_Item1.Explanation: Tries are specifically designed to store words, making them ideal for implementing autocomplete features, allowing fast lookup and retrieval.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which tree-based index structure is widely used in databases to improve query performance?
m_Item1.Explanation: B+ Trees are commonly used as an index structure in databases due to their efficient use of disk storage and their ability to maintain sorted data for fast retrieval.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which node in a binary tree has no parent?
m_Item1.Explanation: The root node is the topmost node in a tree and does not have a parent.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a binary tree, what is the maximum number of children a node can have?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a binary tree, each node can have at most two children.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a tree called if all levels except possibly the last are completely filled, and all nodes are as far left as possible?
m_Item1.Explanation: A complete binary tree is one in which all levels are fully filled except possibly the last, where all nodes are as far left as possible.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which traversal method visits the root of a binary tree before the left and right subtrees?
m_Item1.Explanation: Pre-order traversal visits the root node first, followed by left subtree, and then right subtree.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How is the depth of a node defined in a tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: The depth of a node in a tree is defined as the number of edges from the node to the tree's root node.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What describes a leaf node in a tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: A leaf node is a node that does not have any children.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which tree traversal method uses a queue to process nodes at each level of depth?
m_Item1.Explanation: Level-order traversal processes nodes level by level using a queue for storage.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the height of a tree with a single node?
m_Item1.Explanation: The height of a tree with a single node is 0 since there are no edges.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which term describes a binary tree in which the height of the left and right subtrees of every node differ by no more than one?
m_Item1.Explanation: A balanced binary tree is one in which the height of the left and right subtrees of any node differ by no more than one.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a valid property of a binary search tree (BST)?

Options.OptionText: The left subtree of a node contains nodes with keys less than the node's key.
The right subtree of a node contains nodes with keys greater than the node's key.
Each node can have at most two children.
Every level of the tree is filled completely.

m_Item1.Explanation: A binary search tree does not require every level to be completely filled. That is a property of a complete binary tree.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following binary tree:
  A
 / \
B  C
/ \  \
D E  F
What is the in-order traversal of the tree?

Options.OptionText: D, B, E, A, C, F
A, B, D, E, C, F
D, E, B, F, C, A
A, B, E, D, C, F

m_Item1.Explanation: In-order traversal for a binary tree involves visiting the left subtree first, then the root node, and finally the right subtree. Therefore, the in-order traversal for this tree is D, B, E, A, C, F.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a binary search tree (BST), which traversal method would you use to get the elements in non-decreasing order?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a binary search tree, an in-order traversal retrieves the nodes in non-decreasing order. This is because for any node, the left child is less than the node, and the right child is greater.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given a binary tree, which traversal would visit nodes in the order of: Root, Left, Right?
m_Item1.Explanation: Pre-order traversal visits nodes in the order: Root, Left, Right. This means we visit the root node first, followed by the left subtree, and finally the right subtree.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
For the following tree:
        G
       / \
     I     H
   /      /
 J        K
What is the post-order traversal?
m_Item1.Explanation: Post-order traversal visits nodes in the order: Left, Right, Root. Therefore, the post-order traversal for the given tree is J, I, K, H, G.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about level-order traversal is true?

Options.OptionText: It can be performed recursively like in-order traversal.
It is equivalent to pre-order traversal for a fully balanced tree.
It uses a queue to keep track of nodes at the current level.
It visits nodes in the order of Left, Right, Root.

m_Item1.Explanation: Level-order traversal is typically implemented using a queue to keep track of nodes at the current level. Nodes are visited level by level, from left to right.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary characteristic of a depth-first search (DFS) when traversing a tree structure?

Options.OptionText: It explores all neighbors of a given node first before moving to the next node.
It uses a queue data structure to manage nodes.
It explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
It is optimal and guaranteed to find the shortest path in weighted trees.

m_Item1.Explanation: Depth-first search explores as far along a branch as possible before backtracking. This characteristic distinguishes it from other traversal methods that might explore every node at a given depth before going deeper.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When implementing DFS for a binary tree in Java, which of the following data structures is most commonly used to manage the nodes?
m_Item1.Explanation: A stack data structure is typically used in depth-first search for tracking nodes to visit, as it allows for backtracking by keeping track of the nodes yet to be fully explored.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following methods is NOT typically used to perform a depth-first search on a binary tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: Breadth-first traversal (or breadth-first search) is not a depth-first search method. It involves exploring all nodes at the present depth level before moving onto nodes at the next depth level.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does depth-first search differ from breadth-first search when implemented iteratively?

Options.OptionText: DFS uses more memory compared to BFS.
DFS relies on a stack, whereas BFS relies on a queue.
BFS is better for pathfinding, while DFS ensures completeness.
DFS explores nodes level by level like BFS.

m_Item1.Explanation: In iterative implementations, DFS typically utilizes a stack to explore nodes deeply before backtracking, while BFS uses a queue to explore all nodes at the current depth before moving to the next level.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which traversal order does a depth-first search follow when using the pre-order strategy in a binary tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: Pre-order traversal visits the root node before its left and right subtrees. This order results in a traversal sequence of Root, Left, Right for each subtree.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the following binary tree, what is the inorder traversal sequence?
1
/ \
2 3
/
4
m_Item1.Explanation: In inorder traversal, nodes are recursively visited in this order: left, root, right. Thus, the sequence is 4, 2, 1, 3.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the inorder traversal of the tree represented by the array [15, 10, 25, 5, 12, 20, 30] assuming it's a complete binary tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: For a complete binary tree, the inorder traversal visits nodes in left-root-right order, yielding the sequence 5, 10, 12, 15, 20, 25, 30.

m_Item1.QuestionText: For the binary search tree constructed from the numbers [8, 3, 10, 1, 6, 9, 14], what is the inorder traversal?
m_Item1.Explanation: In BST, inorder traversal yields sorted order: 1, 3, 6, 8, 9, 10, 14.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a binary tree has an inorder traversal of [D, B, E, A, F, C], what is the position of the root node A in this sequence?
m_Item1.Explanation: In an inorder traversal, the root is positioned between the left subtree and the right subtree, making its position fourth in this sequence.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of a binary tree, what is the correct sequence for a preorder traversal?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a preorder traversal, nodes are visited in the following order: Root, Left subtree, Right subtree.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following methods correctly implements a preorder traversal in Java for a binary tree?

Options.OptionText: void preorder(Node node) { if(node != null) { preorder(node.left); System.out.print(node.data + ' '); preorder(node.right); } }
void preorder(Node node) { if(node != null) { System.out.print(node.data + ' '); preorder(node.left); preorder(node.right); } }
void preorder(Node node) { while(node != null) { System.out.print(node.data + ' '); preorder(node.left); node = node.right; } }
void preorder(Node node) { if(node != null) { preorder(node.right); System.out.print(node.data + ' '); preorder(node.left); } }

m_Item1.Explanation: Option B correctly implements a preorder traversal, visiting the root node first, then recursively the left subtree, followed by the right subtree.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given a binary tree of height 3, how many nodes are visited during a preorder traversal if every node has 0 or 2 children?
m_Item1.Explanation: A full binary tree of height 3 has 2^3 - 1 = 7 nodes, all of which are visited in a preorder traversal.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What property is preserved by a preorder traversal but not necessarily by an inorder or postorder traversal?

Options.OptionText: The balanced property of the tree
Visiting nodes in the order as if each is pushed onto a stack
Satisfaction of the binary search tree property

m_Item1.Explanation: A preorder traversal visits nodes in the order that reflects their pushing onto a hypothetical stack, as it very rigidly follows Root, Left, Right sequence.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which traversal method represents the postorder traversal in a binary tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a postorder traversal, the nodes are recursively visited in this sequence: left subtree, right subtree, and then the root node.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given a binary tree with the root node having a left child and a right child, where the left child also has left and right children, what is the postorder traversal sequence?
m_Item1.Explanation: Postorder traversal visits the left subtree first, then the right subtree, and finally the root node.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the postorder traversal of the following tree: 1 is root; 1 has 2 as left child and 3 as right child; 2 has 4 as left child and 5 as right child?
m_Item1.Explanation: The sequence of nodes in postorder traversal is determined by first visiting all the nodes of the left subtree, then the nodes of the right subtree, followed by the root node.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a binary search tree (BST), which of the following traversal would result in nodes being visited in the order of an increasing sequence?
m_Item1.Explanation: Inorder traversal of a binary search tree visits nodes in ascending order of their values. This is different from postorder traversal, which visits left, right, and then root node.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What data structure is typically used to implement Breadth First Search (BFS) on a tree in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: A queue is used in BFS to explore each level of the tree before moving to the next level, ensuring nodes are explored level by level.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In BFS traversal, which of the following provides the correct order of nodes visited in a tree starting from node A?
m_Item1.Explanation: BFS is synonymous with level-order traversal in a tree, where nodes are visited level by level from top to bottom.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is TRUE about Breadth First Search?

Options.OptionText: BFS uses recursion to explore the tree.
BFS can find the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
BFS can be implemented using a stack data structure.
BFS is more memory-efficient than Depth First Search.

m_Item1.Explanation: BFS explores nodes layer by layer, which allows it to find the shortest path in terms of the number of edges in an unweighted graph.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the time complexity of Breadth First Search when implemented on a tree with 'n' nodes?
m_Item1.Explanation: BFS explores each node once, and hence the time complexity is O(n), where 'n' is the number of nodes in the tree.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which Java collection framework class is most suitable for implementing the queue needed for BFS traversal?
m_Item1.Explanation: The LinkedList class in Java implements the Queue interface, making it suitable for BFS which requires queue operations such as enqueue and dequeue.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which traversal method corresponds to a Depth First Search when applied to a binary tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: Inorder, Preorder, and Postorder traversal methods are forms of Depth First Search applied to a binary tree. Levelorder is a Breadth First Search.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given a binary tree, which of the following Java methods correctly implements a DFS in Preorder?

Options.OptionText: public void preorder(Node node) { if(node != null) { System.out.print(node.data + ' '); preorder(node.left); preorder(node.right); } }
public void preorder(Node node) { if(node != null) { preorder(node.left); preorder(node.right); System.out.print(node.data + ' '); } }
public void preorder(Node node) { if(node != null) { preorder(node.left); System.out.print(node.data + ' '); preorder(node.right); } }
public void preorder(Node node) { if(node != null) { preorder(node.right); preorder(node.left); System.out.print(node.data + ' '); } }

m_Item1.Explanation: Preorder traversal visits nodes in the order: root, left, right. Option A adheres to this order.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How would you modify a Depth First Search to find the maximum value in a binary tree?

Options.OptionText: Modify the DFS to keep track of the maximum value found at each node.
Use DFS but stop as soon as a node with data greater than root is found.
Modify the DFS to print the maximum value at the end.
Use BFS instead, which naturally finds the maximum value.

m_Item1.Explanation: A DFS can be modified to check each node's value and update the maximum value found so far, ensuring a complete traversal of the tree.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which data structure is primarily used to implement a non-recursive DFS of a binary tree in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: A Stack is used in non-recursive implementations of DFS to keep track of nodes to visit next, reflecting the call stack of the recursive implementation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What condition should be checked in a recursive DFS implementation to avoid infinite loops when traversing a graph?

Options.OptionText: Check if all nodes are visited
Check if the current node is already visited
Check if the tree contains any cycles
Check if the search depth exceeds a certain limit

m_Item1.Explanation: In a DFS of a graph, each node must be marked as visited to prevent revisiting it, which can cause infinite loops, especially in cyclic graphs.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given a binary tree with the following structure, what will be the result of the inorder traversal?
 1
/ \
2 3
/ \
4 5
m_Item1.Explanation: Inorder traversal visits nodes in the order: left-child, current node, right-child. Thus, for the given tree, the order is 4, 2, 5, 1, 3.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following code snippets correctly implements an inorder traversal of a BinaryTree in Java?

Options.OptionText: public void inorder(Node root) { if (root == null) return; inorder(root.right); System.out.print(root.data); inorder(root.left); }
public void inorder(Node root) { if (root == null) return; System.out.print(root.data); inorder(root.left); inorder(root.right); }
public void inorder(Node root) { if (root == null) return; inorder(root.left); System.out.print(root.data); inorder(root.right); }
public void inorder(Node root) { if (root == null) return; inorder(root.left); inorder(root.right); System.out.print(root.data); }

m_Item1.Explanation: Option C is the correct implementation of inorder traversal which follows the left, root, right sequence in a binary tree.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will the inorder traversal of the following skewed binary tree output?
    4
     \
      5
        \
         6
m_Item1.Explanation: In a right-skewed tree, the inorder traversal processes nodes in an ascending sequence from the root to the deepest right node.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a Binary Search Tree (BST), what property does performing an inorder traversal reveal?

Options.OptionText: The traversal collects the elements in a descending sorted order.
The traversal reflects the maximum height of the tree.
The traversal produces elements in ascending sorted order.
The traversal provides details of the tree's balance.

m_Item1.Explanation: Inorder traversal of a Binary Search Tree provides the nodes in an ascending order due to the tree's ordering property: left < root < right.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following code snippets correctly implements a preorder traversal of a binary tree in Java?

Options.OptionText: void preorder(TreeNode node) { if (node != null) { System.out.print(node.data + " "); preorder(node.left); preorder(node.right); } }
void preorder(TreeNode node) { if (node == null) return; preorder(node.left); System.out.print(node.data + " "); preorder(node.right); }
void preorder(TreeNode node) { while (node != null) { System.out.print(node.data + " "); node = node.left; } }
void preorder(TreeNode node) { for (int i = 0; i < 2; i++) { System.out.print(node.data + " "); if (i == 0) node = node.left; else node = node.right; } }

m_Item1.Explanation: The correct implementation for a preorder traversal visits the root node first, followed by the left subtree, and finally the right subtree. Option A correctly prints the node data, then recursively calls preorder on the left and right children.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
If the root node is null, what will the following preorder traversal method do?
void preorder(TreeNode node) {
    if (node != null) {
        System.out.print(node.data + " ");
        preorder(node.left);
        preorder(node.right);
    }
}

Options.OptionText: It will enter an infinite loop.

m_Item1.Explanation: The method checks if the node is null at the beginning. If it is null, the method will simply return and do nothing.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which part of a binary tree does the preorder traversal visit first?
m_Item1.Explanation: In preorder traversal, the root node is visited first, followed by the nodes in the left subtree, and then the nodes in the right subtree.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following code?
class TreeNode {
    int data;
    TreeNode left, right;
    TreeNode(int item) { data = item; left = right = null; }
}
void preorder(TreeNode node) {
    if (node != null) {
        System.out.print(node.data + " ");
        preorder(node.left);
        preorder(node.right);
    }
}
TreeNode root = new TreeNode(1);
root.left = new TreeNode(2);
root.right = new TreeNode(3);
root.left.left = new TreeNode(4);
root.left.right = new TreeNode(5);
preorder(root);
m_Item1.Explanation: The preorder traversal of the given binary tree visits nodes in the order: root (1), left subtree (2, 4, 5), and then right subtree (3). Therefore, the output is '1 2 4 5 3'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true regarding the preorder traversal using an iterative approach?

Options.OptionText: A stack is used to simulate the recursive calls.
A queue is necessary to maintain the nodes.
Depth-first search techniques do not use stacks in practice.

m_Item1.Explanation: In an iterative implementation of preorder traversal, a stack is used to keep track of nodes whose children need to be processed, effectively simulating recursive behavior.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the following tree, what is the postorder traversal?
 1
/ \
2 3
/ \
4 5
m_Item1.Explanation: In postorder traversal, you first traverse the left subtree, then the right subtree, and finally the root node. So, the correct order is 4, 5, 2, 3, 1.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the result of the postorder traversal for the given binary tree?
         10
       /      \
     20     30
         \    /
         40 50
m_Item1.Explanation: Postorder traversal visits the left subtree first (20), then the right (40), and then the root for the left child. For the right child, left (50) is visited, then the root (30), and finally, the main root (10). So, the correct traversal is 40, 20, 50, 30, 10.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider a binary tree implemented using the node structure below. Which of the following is the postorder traversal order?
Node root = new Node(1);
root.left = new Node(2);
root.right = new Node(3);
root.left.left = new Node(4);
root.left.right = new Node(5);
m_Item1.Explanation: For the given tree, postorder traversal involves visiting the left subtree first (4, 5), then the right subtree (3), and finally the root node (1). Hence, the correct order is 4, 5, 2, 3, 1.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the postorder traversal of a tree that has only a single node with the value '50'?
m_Item1.Explanation: A tree with only one node will have its value as the postorder traversal result, because there are no left or right children to visit.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following defines the postorder traversal for a skewed binary tree with nodes 1 -> 2 -> 3 (only right skewed)?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a right-skewed tree, postorder traversal involves visiting all nodes in the order from the deepest child to the root which results in 3, 2, 1.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What data structure is typically used to implement Breadth First Search (BFS) in a tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: Breadth First Search uses a Queue to keep track of the nodes that need to be explored at each level before moving on to the next level.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the following tree:
1
/ \
2 3
    
What is the Breadth First Search traversal of this tree?
m_Item1.Explanation: BFS traversal visits all nodes at the current depth level before moving on to the nodes at the next depth level. Hence, for the given tree, the traversal order is [1, 2, 3].

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following BFS implementation code in Java. Identify the output for a tree with root being 10 and two children 7 and 15:
public void bfs(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return;
    Queue<TreeNode> queue = new LinkedList<>();
    queue.add(root);
    while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
        TreeNode node = queue.poll();
        System.out.print(node.val + " ");
        if (node.left != null) queue.add(node.left);
        if (node.right != null) queue.add(node.right);
    }
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The BFS implementation uses a queue to visit each level of the tree from left to right. Starting from root 10, it visits 7, followed by 15.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following properties does Breadth First Search of a binary tree ensure?

Options.OptionText: All siblings are visited before moving to the next level
All nodes are visited in alphabetical order
All parent nodes are visited after their children
All leaf nodes are visited before root node

m_Item1.Explanation: BFS ensures that all nodes at the present depth are visited before moving on to nodes at the next depth level, which implies visiting all siblings before the next level.
